entry.title: CVI conversion - quality checks for existing BP
entry.content:
Dear Experts,

We are doing brownfield conversion ECC to S/4 HANA. As part of preparation we run CVI_PRECHK report to check data quality for customers not created as business partners.  

However we have already created Business partners in the system (due to collection module requirement). 

We would like to run quality checks (relevant for CVI_PRECHK) as well for them. However CVI_PRECHK is taking into consideration only customers not mapped to the BP.

Is there a way to run the quality checks for customers/vendors already converted to BP?

Best regards,

Dorota

entry.title: How to enrich values in sorted table in loop-methods
entry.content:
Sometimes we need to loop over table and enriching the values via different methods. For SORTED tables we can not pass the line via CHANGING as it leads to runtime error MOVE_TO_LIT_NOTALLOWED_NODATA.

The code below (full code is here) will lead to runtime error (as on the screenshot)

 

 

CLASS lcl_app DEFINITION.
  PUBLIC SECTION.
    METHODS main.
  PROTECTED SECTION.
  PRIVATE SECTION.
    TYPES: BEGIN OF ts_name_val
        , key_name TYPE string
        , val TYPE string
        , add_value TYPE string
      ,  END OF ts_name_val
      , tt_name_val TYPE STANDARD TABLE OF ts_name_val WITH DEFAULT KEY
      , tt_name_val_srt TYPE SORTED TABLE OF ts_name_val WITH UNIQUE KEY key_name
      .

    METHODS fill_add_value
      CHANGING cs_name_val TYPE ts_name_val.

ENDCLASS.

CLASS lcl_app IMPLEMENTATION.
  METHOD main.
    DATA lt_name_val TYPE tt_name_val_srt.
    FIELD-SYMBOLS <fs_name_val> TYPE ts_name_val.

    lt_name_val = value #(
    ( key_name = 'NUM100' val = 'base_val_100' )
    ( key_name = 'NUM102' val = 'base_val_102' )
    ).

    LOOP AT lt_name_val ASSIGNING <fs_name_val>.
      fill_add_value( CHANGING cs_name_val = <fs_name_val> ).
    ENDLOOP.

  ENDMETHOD.

  METHOD fill_add_value.
    cs_name_val-add_value = 'some val'.
  ENDMETHOD.
ENDCLASS.

START-OF-SELECTION.
  new lcl_app( )->main( ).

 

 

 

However instead of changing we could pass as ref via importing parameter. The code without runtime errors is below (full code could be found here).

 

 

CLASS lcl_app DEFINITION.

  PUBLIC SECTION.
    METHODS main.
  PROTECTED SECTION.

  PRIVATE SECTION.
    TYPES: BEGIN OF ts_name_val
        , key_name TYPE string
        , val TYPE string
        , add_value TYPE string
      ,  END OF ts_name_val
      , tt_name_val TYPE STANDARD TABLE OF ts_name_val WITH DEFAULT KEY
      , tt_name_val_srt TYPE SORTED TABLE OF ts_name_val WITH UNIQUE KEY key_name
      .

    METHODS fill_add_value
      CHANGING cs_name_val TYPE ts_name_val.

    METHODS fill_add_value_ref
      IMPORTING isr_name_val TYPE REF TO ts_name_val.
ENDCLASS.

CLASS lcl_app IMPLEMENTATION.
  METHOD main.
    DATA lt_name_val TYPE tt_name_val_srt.
    FIELD-SYMBOLS <fs_name_val> TYPE ts_name_val.

    lt_name_val = VALUE #(
    ( key_name = 'NUM100' val = 'base_val_100' )
    ( key_name = 'NUM102' val = 'base_val_102' )
    ).

*    LOOP AT lt_name_val ASSIGNING <fs_name_val>.
*      fill_add_value( CHANGING cs_name_val = <fs_name_val> ).
*    ENDLOOP.

    LOOP AT lt_name_val ASSIGNING <fs_name_val>.
      fill_add_value_ref( isr_name_val = REF #( <fs_name_val> ) ).
    ENDLOOP.

  ENDMETHOD.

  METHOD fill_add_value.
    cs_name_val-add_value = 'some val'.
  ENDMETHOD.

  METHOD fill_add_value_ref.
    "IMPORTING isr_name_val TYPE REF TO ts_name_val.
    isr_name_val->add_value = 'some val'.
  ENDMETHOD.
ENDCLASS.

START-OF-SELECTION.
  NEW lcl_app( )->main( ).

 

 

But how are you using do enrich sorted table? via object attribute? Please comment and share you knowledge!
Also I want to highlight that there is a difference between Compiler analyzer and Runtime-analyzer and the only tool to deal with that - it is unit tests or component tests. Hope I could do some blogging about that 😉

Thanks for your attention! 🙂

entry.title: Joule is Now Integrated into SAP Build Work Zone!
entry.content:
We’re thrilled to announce that Joule, SAP’s generative AI assistant, is now integrated into SAP Build Work Zone standard edition, SAP Start, and SAP Mobile Start. This marks a significant step in empowering users to work faster and smarter, with seamless access to insights and information at their fingertips.

Integrating Joule into SAP Build Work Zone brings a unique value to users by effortlessly connecting data from multiple backend systems into a single interface. By providing answers and insights from any supported system, Joule eliminates the need to switch between tools. This empowers users to stay informed with all relevant information and applications in one place.

What is Joule? 

Joule is SAP’s cutting-edge generative AI assistant, designed to bring intelligence and efficiency to every aspect of work. Joule helps users can interact naturally, ask questions, and get precise answers related to their business processes. For instance, end users can inquire about the status of a purchase order and be guided directly to it, streamlining their workflow and saving valuable time.

Joule isn’t just a productivity tool; it’s a game-changer for decision making and operational efficiency. It makes everyday tasks easier and more intuitive for end users.

 Very recently, Joule turned 1! Learn more about Joule here.

BIG NEWS: Joule in SAP Build Work Zone, standard edition

With our latest release, Joule is now integrated into SAP Build Work Zone, standard edition. This enables end users to interact with Joule and get answers from all connected systems​.

This boosts productivity by leveraging Joule intuitive capabilities with SAP Build Work Zone, standard edition. Users can navigate quickly to the functionality needed for their next task, receive precise answers on how to use SAP applications, and complete tasks effortlessly using natural language requests. It can help navigate to relevant applications and perform transactions. Additionally, Joule empowers more informed decision making with answers derived directly from your business document.

Joule can also serve as your personal guide within SAP Build Work Zone, helping you navigate and make the most of your site. Whether you need assistance with managing content or accessing specific features, Joule provides step-by-step guidance.

Here is an example of how you can use Joule within your Work Zone site: Imagine you're trying to track the status of a purchase order. Instead of navigating through multiple screens, you simply ask Joule, “Has my purchase order been approved?”. Joule instantly provides the status and key details directly within its window in SAP Build Work Zone, standard edition. This seamless interaction eliminates the need to switch between applications, saving time and effort.

Prerequisites for Using Joule in SAP Build Work Zone

To begin leveraging Joule in your SAP Build Work Zone environment, ensure the following prerequisites are met:

  • Joule requires Identity Authentication for user login. If you haven’t already switched to SAP Cloud Identity Services - Identity Authentication, this step must be completed beforehand. For further guidance, see Switching to SAP Cloud Identity Services - Identity Authentication.
  • Before completing the Joule onboarding tasks, it should be ensured that he required prerequisites for both SAP Build Work Zone, standard edition and for Joule have been completed. 
  • Run the Joule booster to activate the integration and complete the setup process as seen in the steps below.

Set up subaccount by selecting the subaccount that should be used for Joule provisioning and configurations. 

Selecting the SAP solutions where Joule should be enabled.

Selecting the SAP solutions whose capability packages should be provisioned in Joule.

How to Get Started with the Integration

Integrating Joule into your SAP Build Work Zone is straightforward. After successfully running the Joule booster and completing the integration between SAP Build Work Zone, standard edition and Joule, a new setting becomes available in the Site Setting screen of your site.

 As an administrator, navigate to the Site Settings screen and enable the Joule option under the Services section. Once enabled, the Joule icon will appear in the header of your runtime site, giving users instant access to the AI-powered assistant for enhanced productivity and elevated outcomes across business.

Looking Ahead: What’s Next?

The integration of Joule into SAP Build Work Zone, advanced edition is on the horizon and is coming in 2025. It will bring the Gen AI-powered experiences to an even broader set of users. The advanced edition will offer a wider scope with additional capabilities, tailored to meet diverse business needs. This expansion ensures that every end user can fully harness generative AI for greater efficiency and smarter decision-making.

Joule’s integration into SAP Build Work Zone is more than an enhancement—it's a transformation. It combines the power of SAP Build Work Zone with Joule's intelligence, letting users focus on what matters most. While the complexities of business processes are handled by a trusted AI assistant.

Stay tuned for latest updates as we bring even more innovation to your SAP Build experience.

***

Learn more:

➤ Announcement blog for Joule in SAP Mobile Start

➤ SAP Build Work Zone on: sap.com / SAP Community

➤ SAP Builders Community Group

➤ More information on new product features: Roadmap

entry.title: Obsolete syntax: the dangers of using COMMON PART
entry.content:
Recently i stumbled across a strange issue, that took me quite a long time to understand. Working on a customer development in ABAP, i needed to show available quantity(ATP) per storage location and LE delivery and then update one single field(storage location) on some LE delivery documents. The approach i took was to re-use(call) a couple of standard function modules, that i spotted during debugging the VL03 processing. The execution flow seemed correct, but i was noticing in the debugger that global variables defined in includes were having unexpected values. Mostly it was the internal tables xlips and ylips that are declared in the include VBLPDATA

The issue that i experienced was that the tables were empty. In comparison, during the seemingly same call sequence done from the transaction VL03n, they had values.

After some serious head scratching,  i read the documentation... Of the funny BEGIN OF COMMON PART syntax. It says the following

 

Now if you are like me, you don't think about program GROUPS often, or you maybe not even know what that is. Thankfully, there is this great blog from Olga Dolinskaja - New ABAP Debugger Tipps and Tricks - SAP Community
The debugger feature

" Loaded Programs shows you two tabs. On the Loaded Programs tab you see the attributes of all loaded programs and their program groups. "

was exactly what i needed.

I searched for the include in question, and was able to see that it was loaded in a different program group.

In plain words, the function module where i had issues, was not the first one using this INCLUDE. With the help of the debugger, i was able to see the program group where the table had been loaded to, and this was actually a different function module.

At the end i had to encapsulate my logic in a NEW TASK, so that i was absolutely the common data was being loaded in the right program group. In my case,  CALL FUNCTION IN UPDATE TASK would also have been an alternative.

Conclusion: please never, ever use the COMMON part syntax(it is obsolete anyway). Troubleshooting quickly becomes very difficult. Also, maybe familiarize yourselves with the loaded programs tab in the debugger. The knowledge can be quite useful, in the long run.

entry.title: News in XS Advanced 1.3.0 for SAP HANA 2 SPS08
entry.content:
Reverse Proxy Setup With External Domains

XS Advanced 1.3.0 introduces external domains to simplify the way how you setup a reverse proxy or SAP HANA system replication with XS Advanced. Using external domains to setup a reverse proxy has many advantages:

  • The setup procedure and certificate setup is less complex.
  • Users can login from both, the external domain (e.g. business users) or the default domain (e.g. administrators).
  • The (internal) default domain and the external domain can be in different DNS zones. Hence, you don't need to expose your internal SAP HANA host names to business users.
  • It's now possible to use different port numbers internally and externally. For example, this allows you to expose XS Advanced applications to business users much more easily on the default SSL port 443. 
  • XS Advanced is able to operate internally even when the external route is not available.

This is in particular useful in a SAP HANA System Replication setup, where the startup of the system after a failover is not dependent on the availability of the external route anymore, as illustrated in the following diagram:

In the setup shown above, the default domain setting of the secondary system stays stable on both systems, even after a failover. As a result, both systems can operate independently, and the systems are self-contained after you turn off system replication.

To find out more about the new reverse proxy setup, check the following links:

  • Using A Reverse Proxy with an External Domain 
  • SAP HANA System Replication Setup for XS Advanced Runtime
  • Creating an External Domain 

 

Keep Application Runtimes up to Date

It's now easier to keep application runtimes up to date and remove outdated application runtimes! For example, XS Advanced provides the following enhancements to the application-runtime maintenance commands:

The following example shows how to use the xs runtimes command to see which application runtimes are outdated:

And you can run the command xs restage --outdated to update affected applications with newer application runtimes:

Use xs delete-runtime --outdated to get rid of outdated, unused application runtimes, as illustrated in the following example:

You have now successfully updated applications to the latest application runtime version and removed old application runtimes from the system, as you can see by running the xs runtimes command again:

  

Get Ready for Java 21 and Node.js 22

  • We added support for Java 21 with SapMachine 21 and Node.js 22 to enable you to write modern, up-to-date application code.
  • Java 11 with SapMachine 11 runs out of maintenance and, for this reason, is not contained in the XS advanced shipment any longer. Make sure you update your applications to Java 17 or higher!

 

Renovations Under The Hood

We have also worked hard on renovating the XS Advanced platform under the hood, for example:

  • We have tidied up the XS Advanced installation directory to reduce the number of files and disk usage.
  • We have improved the performance of the system startup and maintenance commands.
  • We have fixed functional and security issues and improved supportability of the product.

 

Improved Features in XS Advanced Cockpit

We have worked on improving the XS Advanced Cockpit with regards to user experience, such as more convenience for keyboard users, and also have added useful new features:

System Overview

XS Advanced Cockpit 1.1.43 provides a System Overview page that displays a summary of the connected XS Advanced system which includes the current health status, as shown in the following screenshot:

Improved Application Log Viewer

The Application Log Viewer now shows complete log lines as well as human-readable timestamps, includes improved filtering and exporting capabilities, and it is now much easier to set and view logging levels:

Application Runtime Management

You can now see which application runtimes are present in the system and which runtimes need maintenance:

You can delete application runtimes that are out of maintenance, temporarily disable runtimes, or navigate to a page showing detailed information, for example, the applications that are bound to a runtime.

Simplified Maintenance of Space and Org Members

Adding space and organization members now is much more convenient! The corresponding dialog shows all available origins and requires less steps to add new users:

  • For users managed by SAP HANA, the required role collections are automatically assigned.
  • For users managed by an SAML IdP, new users are automatically added to the UAA.

Export and Import Role-Collection Mappings

When configuring an SAML IdP for XS Advanced, you can now export to a file the mappings between SAML groups and XS Advanced role collections, for example, for the purpose of a backup, or if you want to import the same mappings for a different IdP or even a different system:

Refreshed XS Advanced Job Scheduler UI

We've revised the Job Scheduler's UI theme. We've shifted the visual appeal to a fresher and more up-to-date theme. The design is intended to enhance the appearance, the general user-experience, and the usability of the application.

We've also modified the table types for jobs and schedules, with the aim of improving the accessibility, comprehension, and management of job and schedule data. Altogether, this helps to make the user experience more effortless and efficient.

The active and inactive indicators for jobs and schedules have also been revamped and redesigned to simplify the navigation process. Lastly, we have improved the stability of the product.

entry.title: How to Monitor IBM Db2 LUW Database Performance - Part 2: DBA Cockpit
entry.content:
Wouldn’t it be great…

  • if you could go back in time to see how your Db2 database performed several days ago?
  • if you could use graphical screens to diagnose your Db2 performance and workload?
  • if all this was possible without having to take care of how to collect the required monitoring data because there’s a convenient SAP tool that does it for you?

This blog post is part 2 to my previous blog post “How to Monitor Db2 Database Performance – MONREPORT Module” where I explained the monitor elements provided by the Db2 for LUW database and showed you how you can use the time-spent monitor elements to analyze your Db2 system performance. While in part 1 I demonstrated how the Db2 monitor elements can be queried from the command line, I will now give you in part 2 a closer look on the DBA Cockpit.

 

DBA Cockpit (SAP transaction DBACOCKPIT)

The DBA Cockpit regularly collects different kinds of monitoring data, stores the monitoring data in the database, and provides graphical screens to analyze it. This allows you to go back in time and to see how your database performance was, for example, several days ago. Based on what I discussed in part 1, I will show you now how the same Db2 monitor elements are used by the DBA Cockpit. The knowledge about time-spent monitor elements from part 1 will help you to interpret the monitoring data collected by the DBA Cockpit.

System and Activity Dimension

Remember the main aspect discussed in part 1: The Db2 time spent elements can be divided into two different dimensions. While the first dimension looks on the overall system performance, the second dimension provides performance information for single activities. For Db2, activities are mainly SQL statements processed on the database. To analyze a Db2 performance issue and to further narrow it down, you need both dimensions which you can find in the DBA Cockpit as follows:
Call transaction DBACOCKPIT and go to the screen Performance -> Time Spent Analysis where you can find the system dimension. In addition, use Performance -> Top SQL Statements or, for an ad-hoc analysis, also the Performance -> SQL Cache screen in the DBA Cockpit to get information about activity performance.

Comparison of processing times with wait times

In part 1, you learned that the system dimension as well as activity dimension can be observed from two perspectives:
You can either compare the processing time with wait time, or the elapse time with the processing time. The DBA Cockpit uses only the first perspective, that is, it compares processing times with wait times. I will show you this in detail for the different screens.

Note:

The DBA Cockpit uses the term “metric” instead of “monitor element”, which is used in the IBM Db2 documentation. In this blog post, I’ll use both terms as synonyms.

You can find more information about the Db2 monitor elements in the IBM Db2 documentation. For more information about the DBA Cockpit, see https://help.sap.com/docs/r/db6_dbacockpit on SAP Help Portal.

 

DBA Cockpit: Time Spent Analysis

Now, let’s have a look at the screens and functionalities in detail.

Start SAP transaction DBACOCKPIT and go to Performance -> Time Spent Analysis. The information on this screen is mainly collected by the workload manager (WLM) data collectors. You can find and set up all data collectors in the DBA Cockpit under Configuration -> Data Collection Framework Configuration.

By default, the DBA Cockpit keeps the monitoring data of the last 14 days. This means you’re not only able to check the current database performance, but that you can also go back in time and compare, for example, the situation of the current week to the week before.

On the Time Spent Analysis screen, you can specify the following:
In the Time Frame field, you can choose from the dropdown list which time interval you want to analyze. You can use one of the predefined time frames like Last 24 Hours, or you define your own by choosing Custom Selection.

As Display option, you can choose between Aggregate or Timeline.

If you choose Aggregate in the Display field, you will see a graph like the following:

As mentioned before, we’re comparing processing times with wait times. Let’s have a closer look on the example in the figure above and find out if this database has a performance issue. The figure shows the following processing times: Other Section Processing Time (46.8%), Total Backup Processing Time (4.2%), Section Sort Processing Time (1.3%), and Runstats Processing Time (0.8%).

Processing Times

The backup processing time is the time Db2 spent for backing up its data. The RUNSTATS processing time is the time needed to collect new statistics on the database tables and indexes. Other section processing time and section sort processing time are times related to query execution. If you add up all these times, it becomes obvious that the database spent 53% of its time for being busy with processing data and other actions like backup or collecting statistics.

Wait Times

But what about the other 47% of database time? Here we see the wait times, which are Total Extended Latch Wait Time (34.6%), Log Disk Wait Time (2.8%), and Buffer Pool Read Time (1.7%) as well as Prefetch Wait Time (0.8%). If you add them all up, the database spent 40% of its time for waiting. The rest of the time is spent on processing and wait times, which are currently not monitored by a specific metric.

With 40% of the database time needed for waiting for something and especially with nearly 35% of the wait time being spent for waiting on latches, you should have a closer look on the extended latch wait times in this example. Depending on your SAP Basis release, you can find more information about the extended latch wait time under Diagnostics in the DBA Cockpit. I’ll talk about the extended latch wait times in more detail when I come to the SQL queries in the next part of this blog post.

Timeline View

But before, I want to show you another display option on the Time Spent Analysis screen, which is the timeline view. If you choose Timeline in the Display field, you get a graphic like the following:

The timeline view provides you with the same information for the same database metrics as the Aggregate display, but now for each point in time when the data was collected. With this display option, you can identify if the performance stays the same over time or if there are peaks.

Analyze Peaks

A peak can mean that, for example, a certain query or job is causing a performance issue. It can also mean that the performance is only bad during times with a high workload. In the figure above, there’s a peak around 7:30 in the morning, which could point to an issue with the system at this time of day. Therefore, the next step would be to check which jobs, programs, and SQL statements were executed at this time and if the same peak occurs each time these jobs or programs are executed.

Filtering the Service Classes

Another option to narrow down a performance issue is to use a filter in the Service Classes field on the Time Spent Analysis screen:

SAP defines different workload management (WLM) service classes and WLM workloads. This WLM definition is based on SAP ABAP process types like batch or dialog. Depending on the ABAP process type, the Db2 workload is sorted into different WLM service classes, which are available for selection in the dropdown list of the Service Classes field. You can use them as filters for further analyzing performance issues. For example, if a batch job is causing your performance issue, you might find this by filtering for service class SAP_ABAPBTCH_SSC.

 

DBA Cockpit: SQL Cache and Top SQL Statements

While the Time Spent Analysis provides the system dimension, you can analyze the activity dimension on the SQL Cache and Top SQL Statements screens that you both find under Performance in the DBA Cockpit.

If you choose Performance -> SQL Cache, you can check all currently active SQL statements on the database. Under Top SQL Statements, you find the most expensive statements executed during the last days. Both screens are mainly using the Db2 monitoring table function MON_GET_PKG_CACHE_STMT, and Top SQL Statements depends on the DBA Cockpit data collector for the package cache.

Selection Area

On the Top SQL Statements screen, you can define a time frame for which you would like to see the most expensive queries. This works in the same way as for the Time Spent Analysis screen. Again, you can go back in time and check the execution time of SQL statements executed several days ago. The SQL Cache screen provides you with an ad-hoc analysis of the currently executed SQL statements and therefore does not have the option to define a time frame.

On both screens, you can choose by which database metric the top SQL statements will be sorted. You can find the possible metrics in the dropdown list of the Top SQL Statements by field. By default, the top 25 statements with the highest coordinator statement execution time are listed.

You can specify the size of the result list in the field Limit No. of Entries in Result List to. By default, the top 25 SQL statements are listed. If you are searching for specific SQL statements, you can enter your search pattern in field SQL Statement Text (*). For example, if you are looking for all statements accessing table TAB1, you could use “*TAB1*” as search pattern. Furthermore, you can filter the result list for SQL statements with an execution time higher than a given value or for SQL statements with a high average execution time or with a certain number of executions. By default, only dynamic SQL statements are displayed. But if you choose Display Static Packages, also static SQL statements will be listed in the result set.

Adding more columns

For Top SQL Statements as well as for SQL Cache, you can add more columns with monitor elements to the table on the screen. To do so, choose Change Layout from the dropdown menu of the Change Layout button.

A popup appears on which you can change the displayed columns. You can add further columns from the Column Set to display their values or remove them by moving already displayed columns back to the Column Set. Each column represents a Db2 monitor element. You find here the time-spent monitor I’ve already described in part 1 of this blog post mini-series and here in part 2 for the system dimension on the Time Spent Analysis screen in the DBA Cockpit.

You can now decide which monitor elements you want to add. For the first performance checks, you should concentrate on the processing time and wait time monitor elements.

By default, the SQL Cache as well as Top SQL Statements screens show you the statement text, the coordinator statement execution time, number of coordinator statement execution, and the average execution time. This means that you can see how often a certain query was executed (Number of Coordinator Statement Execution) and how long all these executions took (Coordinator Statement Execution Time). With the Average Execution Time, the DBA Cockpit calculates the average execution time of a single SQL statement.

First step: Add total activity time and total activity wait time

As a first step, you can add Total Activity Time and Total Activity Wait Time to these default columns. For dynamic SQL statements, Total Activity Time shows the same value as Coordinator Statement Execution Time. If you compare the Total Activity Wait Time with the Total Activity Time, you can see how much time was spent in executing the SQL statement and for how long Db2 had to wait on something before it could continue processing. Total Activity Wait Time is a subset of Total Activity Time. You can calculate how much time Db2 was actively working on an activity by doing the math (that is, Total Activity Time minus Total Activity Wait Time).

Second step: Narrow it down by adding more columns

If the Total Activity Wait Time is high, you can narrow this wait time further down in a second step by adding more time-spent monitor elements for different wait times. For a SELECT statement, this could be the buffer pool read time, the prefetch wait time, and the extended latch wait time. If you are looking for an INSERT, DELETE, UPDATE, or MERGE statement, the lock-wait time and the log disk wait time could also be helpful.

A high buffer pool read time or a high prefetch wait time could point to a buffer pool that is too small, or to an issue with your I/O subsystem. Db2 latches are used inside Db2 to protect shared resources. It is normal that a Db2 database uses latches. But in systems with a high concurrency, it can be useful to look for latch overheads, which will be reported by the extended latch wait time. If you find a high extended latch wait time, you can get more information about the used latches in the DBA Cockpit under Diagnostics. In most cases, a high extended latch wait time points to a lack of system resources. You can get more information about latches from IBM, for example, on the IBM Db2 Support page.

If you run into high log disk wait times, you should check the disk on which your transaction log files are stored. Lock waits can be analyzed further using the lock event monitor and in the DBA Cockpit using the Diagnostics -> Lock-Wait Events screen.

Access Plan of SQL Statements

If an SQL statement is slow and there aren’t high wait times for it, you should check the access plan of the statement. Maybe the access plan uses a suboptimal join order, or maybe an index is missing. A high CPU time for the query or a high number for Rows Read / Rows Processed can also point to a bad access plan or a missing index.

 

Summary

Db2 provides a high number of monitor elements, which you can query using MON_GET table functions. The DBA Cockpit collects these monitor elements regularly, calculates the delta values between the single collections, and stores the monitoring data for later analysis. The DBA Cockpit makes it easy for you to get an overview of the complete database performance by using the Time Spent Analysis screen. But it is also possible to analyze single SQL statements by using the SQL Cache and Top SQL Statements screens in the DBA Cockpit.

Let me know in the comments what monitor elements you use and if this blog post is - hopefully - helpful for you.

If you’re interested in learning more, check out our SAP on Db2 for LUW learning journey.

entry.title: Restrict budget availability checks by fiscal year for specific company code
entry.content:
Our company code XYZ went live in 2021, with Funds Management activated in 2024. Due to audit requirements, we need to post adjustments for 2023. However, when attempting these postings, an error appears indicating insufficient budget. Despite budgeting being implemented from 2024, the system is checking budget availability for 2023. Is there a way to restrict budget availability checks by fiscal year specifically for company code?

Please note other company codes funds management has been activated from 2020.

entry.title: Re: sp_move_file does not find the file with "č" character in filename
entry.content:
Actually, the strings used in the database are all NVARCHAR. It does not work even if we use N'/disk....'. We noticed, that all the "new" sp_* functions have this problem, but the functions xp_write_file or xp_read_file do not.

CALL xp_write_file('tmp/Testfileč.txt', '123456' ); -- ok

SELECT sp_move_file(N'tmp/Testfileč.txt', N'tmp/Testfileč2.txt'); -- error

entry.title: SAP Integration Architecture Guide
entry.content:
SAP Integration Architecture Guide Update
The SAP Integration Architecture Guide is based on the recent update SAP Integration Architecture Guide explained in a summarized way, emphasizes how organizations can utilize SAP Integration Suite to create a scalable, efficient, and potentially productive integration landscape. This blog will thoroughly explore the essential principles, methods, tools, and practices outlined in the guide, providing a concise insight into SAP’s strategy regarding integration.
________________________________________

 1. Introduction to Integration Architecture
The Integration Architecture Guide emphasizes the increasing significance of integration as a strategic business driver. SAP emphasizes:
• The necessity for smooth connectivity throughout various IT environments, such as hybrid, multi-cloud, and on-premises environments.
• Leveraging SAP Integration Suite as an enterprise-grade iPaaS (Integration Platform as a Service) to connect applications, data, and users effectively.
• Assisting complete processes such as Lead-to-Cash, Hire-to-Retire, and Procure-to-Pay across SAP and non-SAP systems.
________________________________________

2. Core Integration Principles
The guide defines four foundational principles of SAP’s integration strategy:

      

 Predefined Integration/ Out-Of-The-Box Integration
• Prepackaged APIs, integration flows, and domain models facilitate implementation and curtail costs.
• Instances comprise preconfigured connectors for processes such as Invoice Management or else Employee Onboarding.

Open Integration
• Out of the box third-party integration via more than 170 Open Connectors.
• Public APIs for customization and extensibility, establishing compatibility with non-SAP systems.

Holistic Integration
• Process Integration: Automating workflows between SAP and non-SAP applications.
• Data Integration: Providing synchronized and uniform master data through systems.
• User Integration: Delivering flawless user experiences through SAP Build Work Zone.
• Analytics Integration: Utilizing SAP Analytics Cloud for practical understanding AI-Driven Integration.

AI-Driven Integration
• Integration Advisor: A machine learning-powered tool to automate complex mapping tasks and provide integration templates.
• Better efficiency in B2B and API-based integrations.

 

________________________________________

3. SAP Integration Solution Advisory Methodology (ISA-M)
SAP’s ISA-M is a framework for building a robust enterprise integration strategy. It includes:

 

Phase 1: Assess Your Integration Strategy

 

• High level assessment of your integration architecture through integration domains, current scope of integration domains are Cloud2Cloud, Cloud2OnPremise, OnPremise2OnPremise, User2Cloud, User2OnPremise, Thing2Cloud, Thing2OnPremise.    

• Examine existing systems, pinpoint deficiencies, and outline integration areas.
• Categorize integration patterns (A2A, B2B, B2G, etc.) and styles (process, data, user). Typically an integration architect who has a solid understanding of integration requirements across business applications performs the scoping of integration styles.

    

 

Phase 2: Design Hybrid Integration Platform

• Create architectural designs customized for business requirements.
• Establish frameworks and tools for smooth integration through the tools and technology mapping, utilizing SAP Integration Suite

Phase 3: Define Integration Best Practices

 


• Make templates and architecture reusable blueprints as a reference for all Integration developments.
• Align with SAP governance frameworks to ensure compliance and uniformity.

 


Phase 4: Enable a Practice of Empowerment

 


• Establish Centers of Excellence (CoE) to promote innovation and facilitate knowledge sharing through the right organizational structure.

 

 

 

• A set of templates with a governance structure for all the enterprise integration developments, below is an example for reference.

 

                 

________________________________________

4. Big Picture - SAP Integration Suite
SAP Integration Suite serves as a unified integration platform with the following components:

 Cloud Integration

• Core middleware component simplifies the communication through a integrated flow design and used for diverse scenarios (A2A, B2B, B2G).
• Used for both Synchronous and Asynchronous communication minimal latency.
• Prebuilt Prepackaged Content with end-to-end process blueprints are available.
• Integration Advisor and Trading Partner Management through AI/ML learning used to generate mappings and interfaces templates.

API Management
• Serves as the baseline for API lifecycle management along with the industry best practices for security.
• Integrates with Graph to enable seamless access to interconnected data.
Advanced Event Mesh
• Works as the core for event driven architecture enabling asynchronous communication across systems with scalability and system availability options.
• Handles high-volume, low-latency event streams.

        


Dataspace Integration
• Offers secure and collaborative data exchange across systems.
• Supports compliance with data governance standards within industry partnerships like Catena-X.
________________________________________

5. Integration Patterns and Use Cases
The guide categorizes integration use cases into distinct patterns:
Application-to-Application (A2A)
• Example: SAP S/4HANA and SAP SuccessFactors real time synchronization for employee and organizational data.
Business-to-Business (B2B)
• Example: EDI-based order processing and invoicing with trading partners.
Business-to-Government (B2G)
• Example: SAP Document and Reporting Compliance for Automated tax filing as the e-invoicing solution.
Master Data Integration
• Synchronization of master data, such as employees, customers or products, across all connected systems.

________________________________________

6. API Life Cycle
The API lifecycle starts from API planning, creation and implementation by company developers and other API creators and composers, to the consumption of these APIs by other employees, partners or users of the company’s products and services. The figure below illustrates the API lifecycle,

                      ________________________________________

7. SAP Business Accelerator Hub
SAP Business Accelerator Hub works with BTP, it’s a platform that allows users to discover APIs, integration content, business events, and process blueprints to build and extend applications. It enables API-based business functionality (organized into packages) and provides process insights using SAP Enterprise Architecture Methodology. APIs available on Business Accelerator Hub enable developers to build custom integrations, create new applications, and extend the functionality of SAP solutions.
It gives diverse sandbox APIs for several SAP products like SAP S/4HANA, SAP BTP, SAP SuccessFactors, SAP Concur, and more. It can be used to make extensions, applications, or integrations and test them with testing data provided, via these sandbox API.


SAP drives API adoption by:
1. Positioning APIs as digital building blocks.
2. Enhancing API maturity.
3. Providing a lifecycle API platform.
4. Engaging developers via API discovery and consumption.

________________________________________

8. SAP Integration Suite – Hybrid Strategy
The Edge Integration Cell is a hybrid approach that enables the processing of data within a private landscape having Kubernetes container, runtime environment facilitating secure, internal data exchange. Existing integrations can be migrated and run on the Edge Integration Cell with provided tooling.

                  

________________________________________

9. SAP Integration Suite, managed gateway for spend management and SAP Business Network
The SAP Integration Suite, managed gateway for spend management and SAP Business Network, makes integration through its Integration-as-a-Service (IaaS) model. This solution utilizes the Cloud Integration capability, connects buyers' and suppliers' systems with systems like SAP S/4HANA, SAP ECC, Taulia, and SAP Ariba at no extra cost. It offers A2A, B2B, and B2G scenarios, emphasizing processes such as supply chain management, procure-to-pay, and order-to-cash.

 

Key features include:
• Predefined Scenarios: Pre-Packaged mappings and message types.
• Self-Service Tools: Integration project wizards, document validation, mapping tools, and monitoring frameworks.
• Reusable Content: Prebuilt integration flows via SAP Ariba Transformation Service.
The suite enables seamless integration across heterogeneous IT landscapes, supporting event-driven and API-managed models. It provides secure connectivity, robust monitoring, and ensures compliance with regulatory standards. Integration tools like the Edge Integration Cell and SAP Application Interface Framework further enhance flexibility and governance. This hybrid, scalable approach addresses diverse business needs while future-proofing integration strategies.

________________________________________

10. Migration Strategy
The guide emphasizes a structured migration approach to SAP Integration Suite:
• Migration Assessment Tool: Evaluate readiness for transition and identify gaps.
• Prebuilt Migration Packs: Automate repetitive tasks to reduce complexity.
• Hybrid Strategy Support: Ensure continuity by enabling hybrid operations during migration.

SAP is transitioning to a flexible BTP multi-cloud foundation, with BTP Neo set to retire by 2028 and SAP NetWeaver/Process Orchestration by 2027. The focus is on SAP Integration Suite as the primary integration solution.
SAP provides migration support through:
• Neo to Integration Suite:
  o Migration packs for smaller projects and tools for complex scenarios (e.g., tenant cloning and API migration).
  o Best practices, documentation, and cost-lowering guidance.
• Process Orchestration to Integration Suite:
  o Step-wise migration guidance, including free assessments, migration services, and premium offerings.
  o Edge Integration Cell for secure on-premise integration, supporting migrated flows.
SAP’s tools and services ensure a smooth transition with minimized commercial and operational impact.

________________________________________

11. SAP Application Interface Framework (AIF)
SAP Application Interface Framework, embedded in SAP S/4HANA, enhances interface management by enabling efficient implementation, monitoring, and analysis of application interfaces. It integrates deeply with SAP applications, supporting features like data validation, value mappings, and business logic-based checks.
Key benefits include:
• Business user-friendly monitoring, error management, and alerting without IT support.
• GDPR-compliant error handling with in-app corrections, avoiding message resends.
• Role-based governance to secure interface data and hide sensitive fields.
AIF complements SAP Integration Suite by providing robust application-level integration and management capabilities.

                   

________________________________________

12. Technology Guidance for Specialized Integrations
SAP offers specific guidance for various integration scenarios:
• Event-Driven Architecture: Real-time responses to critical system events.
• API-Managed Integration: Comprehensive API lifecycle management to connect internal and external applications.
• B2B Integration: Secure data exchange with partners using protocols like EDI.
• Master Data Integration: Centralized data governance for consistency across platforms.
• Dataspace Integration: Facilitates secure and sovereign data sharing between organizations using SAP's implementation of the Eclipse Dataspace Connector, particularly in consortium-driven contexts like Catena-X Automotive Network.
________________________________________

13. AI-Assisted Tools and Enhancements
Integration Advisor
• Automates mappings for complex integration scenarios through specific templates.
• Leverages machine learning to recommend best practices and streamline B2B integrations.
Business Accelerator Hub
• Repository of prebuilt APIs, integration templates, and business events for rapid deployment.
________________________________________

14. Data Integration Patterns
1. Data Replication:

Migration of data from one system to another keeping the synchronization intact across the landscapes, in such cases usually requires high data availability, backups, or reporting, SAP supports this through tools like SAP HANA Smart Data Integration(SDI) and SLT Replication Server.

2. Data Virtualization:

Provides a unified view of data without replication. It facilitates on-demand access to data from diverse sources using tools like SAP Data Warehouse Cloud and SAP Datasphere.

3. Data Quality Management:

Maintaining a reliable and accurate data set through data cleansing and various validations. SAP's solutions include SAP Data Intelligence and SAP Information Steward to monitor and improve data quality.

4. Data Orchestration:

Coordinates data flow across systems for streamlined processes. It uses SAP Integration Suite for tasks like data transformation, error handling, and real-time processing.

5. Overview of Products and Services for Data Integration:

The SAP Datasphere, SAP Integration Suite, and SAP Data Intelligence are key solutions to handle diverse integration needs, including replication, orchestration, and quality management, for hybrid and multi-cloud environments.

6. Data Integration vs. Process Integration:

Process Integration addresses business process automation and data flow management from one system to another, while Data Integration focuses on data synchronization, governance, and virtualization. SAP offers complementary tools for both styles under its Integration Suite.
________________________________________

15. Analytics Integration in SAP

SAP Analytics integration involves data from various sources towards one combined data set through which insights and decision making is made easier by making one source of truth in order to calculate the key performance indicators. This includes embedded analytics such as reports
and dashboards into a business application and cross-application analytics by combining data of one or more source systems using an analytics or data warehouse solution. On top of the combined data an analytic consumption model can be built to enable (cross app) reporting. For example Order to Cash Dashboard which is embedded in S/4 Hana Cloud, using predefined charts it allows the sales managers to analyze the key figures, whereas, Financial Analytics Dashboard enables a workforce performance analysis, combining the HR  data from Success Factors and Finance data from S/4 Hana making cross application data model and bringing the Cross Application Analytics.


Key Components:
1. Use Case Patterns:
  o Embedded Analytics: Embeds reports and dashboards directly into business applications (e.g., an Order-to-Cash dashboard in SAP S/4HANA Cloud).
  o Cross-Application Analytics: Combines data from multiple sources, such as financial data from SAP S/4HANA and HR data from SAP SuccessFactors, into unified dashboards.

     


2. SAP Analytics Cloud (SAC):
  o Capabilities: Combines business intelligence (BI), planning, and augmented analytics, enhanced with AI and ML.
  o Features:
     - Data exploration, dashboards, enterprise reporting.
     - Predictive analytics and automated insights.
     - Planning tools for budgeting and forecasting.
     - Integration with Microsoft Excel for enhanced usability.
3. SAP Datasphere:
  o Role in Analytics: Provides a data foundation for cross-application analytics, ensuring seamless access to business-critical data with preserved semantics.
  o Key Features:
      Unified data management through cataloging, semantic modeling, federation, and virtualization.
      Accelerates time-to-value by leveraging prebuilt semantic definitions.
      Supports hybrid and cloud environments, enabling complex analytics scenarios.


4. Business Data Fabric:
  o Offers a semantically rich layer across data landscapes, ensuring integrated, consistent, and context-aware data without duplication.
5. Partner Integrations:
  o Collaboration with vendors like Collibra (data cataloging), Confluent (real-time streaming), DataRobot (ML models), and Google Cloud (AI/ML integration).
________________________________________

16. User Integration in SAP
User integration focuses on creating a seamless, unified user experience across systems and applications. Key aspects include:

           


1. Centralized Access:
  o Provides a single entry point to access multiple business applications and processes.
  o Achieved through tools like SAP Build Work Zone, SAP Start, and SAP Mobile Start.
2. Enhanced User Experience (UX):
  o Enables consistent cross-system experiences.
  o Incorporates UX integration qualities, ensuring usability across platforms.
3. Technology Support:
  o Leverages SAP tools for integration to facilitate user-centric designs.
  o Ensures intuitive interfaces, supporting both desktop and mobile users.

Data Provisioning Options
When integrating systems for SAP Front End applications, several data provisioning options are available depending on the deployment scenario:
1. SAP Gateway (On-Premise):
  • Embedded Deployment:
    o Suitable for fully on-premise setups.
    o Simplifies configuration but limits content scope and scalability.
    o Directly integrates UI and backend, offering improved performance.
  • Hub Deployment:
    o Preferred when provisioning data from multiple back-end systems or when using Fiori apps in the cloud.
    o Centralizes configuration, supports global environments, and decouples UI from back-end systems for enhanced scalability and security.
2. SAP OData Provisioning Service (SAP BTP):
  • Neo Environment:
    o Ideal for cloud-based Fiori/ Frontend apps to reduce TCO.
    o Eliminates the need for on-premises hub systems.
    o Suitable for connecting single or multiple back-end systems.
  • Cloud Foundry Environment:
    o Designed for hybrid setups requiring data provisioning from multiple back-end systems.
    o Reduces TCO while supporting SAP Business Suite or SAP S/4HANA systems with OData v2 services.
3. SAP Integration Suite (Cloud Integration Capability):
  • Recommended when data sources are mixed (some OData and others non-OData).
  • Connects on-premises or cloud systems, both SAP and third-party.
4. Deployment Considerations:
  • Hub Deployment:
    o Provides centralized functionality (e.g., in-box, search).
    o Requires synchronization with back-end systems but ensures decoupling for scalability and security.
  • Embedded Deployment:
    o Limits scope and central functionality but simplifies ownership and configuration.


Use Case Patterns for User Integration


1. UI Integration:
  o Description: Combines multiple application UIs into a centralized entry point. Supports backend service consumption through a unified web user interface.
  o Example: Federates content from SAP business applications like SAP S/4HANA Cloud, SAP Integrated Business Planning, and integrates custom or third-party applications.
2. Mobile Integration:
  o Description: Provides access to backend application services via mobile apps, leveraging native or hybrid device capabilities.
  o Example: Mobile entry points for accessing SAP solutions like SAP S/4HANA and SAP SuccessFactors using native applications optimized for mobile platforms.
3. Chatbot Integration:
  o Description: Integrates chatbots into communication channels to interact with backend applications, enabling transactions or information retrieval.
  o Example: SAP Joule integrated with SAP SuccessFactors to facilitate natural language interactions with business users.
These patterns address diverse user needs by enabling seamless access across devices and interfaces, improving efficiency and the user experience.
________________________________________

17. Identity Integration
Identity integration ensures seamless user authentication, role management, and lifecycle management across SAP and third-party applications in both cloud and on-premise landscapes. Key components include:

                   

1. Authentication and Single Sign-On (SSO):
  • Purpose: Simplifies user access with unified login credentials, enhancing security and user convenience.
  • Solutions:
   o SAP Cloud Identity Services - Identity Authentication (IAS):
     Supports SSO for SAP applications.
     Integrates multi-factor authentication for enhanced security.
   o SAP Secure Login Service (SLS):
     Generates short-term user certificates (X.509) for SSO in ABAP systems.
     Facilitates secure access through corporate Identity Providers (IdPs).
  • Heterogeneous Scenarios:
   o Combines cloud and on-premise systems, ensuring smooth authentication with tools like SAP GUI.


2. Identity Lifecycle Management/ Identity Provisioning:

 

 

  • Challenges: Managing identities across applications and landscapes while reducing complexity and ensuring automation.
  • Solutions:
   o SAP Cloud Identity Services:
     Centralizes identity storage, lifecycle management, and replication.
     Provides lightweight tokens for app access, minimizing user replication efforts.
   o SCIM API (Identity Directory Service - IdDS):
     Automate integration with applications.
   o Global User ID:
     Acts as a unique identifier for users across applications, ensuring consistent correlation of tasks and permissions.
3. Roles and Authorization:
  • Purpose: Defines and manages user access levels across applications.
  • Solution:
   o Authorization Management Service:
    - Centralizes policy definitions for new applications.
    - Ensures consistent role and permission management across integrated systems.
    - Facilitates compliance and streamlined governance.


Benefits of Reference Architecture:
  • Centralized Authentication and Identity Flow:
   o Single endpoint for authentication and identity assignment.
  • Simplified Integration:
   o Facilitates onboarding of additional SAP applications.
  • Compliance and Security:
   o Enforces consistent authorization policies across hybrid landscapes.


SAP’s identity integration framework leverages central services like SAP Cloud Identity Services and global identifiers to streamline authentication, lifecycle management, and role assignments, ensuring a cohesive, secure, and scalable identity management strategy.
________________________________________

18. Integration Configuration with the Cloud Integration Automation Service (CIAS)
CIAS provides a centralized framework for defining and automating the configuration of integration scenarios, offering:
1. Standardization: Consistent user experience and standardized configurations for integration scenarios delivered by SAP.
2. Automation: Parts of the integration setup are automated, reducing effort and errors.
3. Customization: Configuration steps are tailored to the customer’s specific cloud and on-premise landscape, with dynamically derived hyperlinks and configuration values.


Integration Scenarios Supported:
• CIAS supports scenarios for end-to-end processes like:
  o Lead to Cash
  o Recruit to Retire
  o Source to Pay
• Integration with tools such as:
  o SAP Integration Suite
  o SAP Cloud Connector
  o SAP Launchpad Service


Two Phases of Configuration:

1. Planning Phase:
  o Select a scenario.
  o Gather the specific config content at one place along with the specific system and tenant data.
  o Prepare a tailored customer specific workflow based on the customer’s landscape.
2. Execution Phase:
  o Specifies the steps for the required scenario for the generation of the workflow
  o Users are guided step-by-step, and tasks are grouped by roles (e.g., "Configuring the BTP ABAP Environment to use the Custom Code Migration App").
  o The guided automation is triggered when required, but the manual interventions is retained for complex and security critical tasks.

The Cloud Integration Automation Service transforms setup into a more guided, and standardized process. For example, For the custom code migration app setup, the CIAS service is used where required in a more effective way which decreases the complexity and enhances the overall process in a more simplified way across hybrid landscapes.

________________________________________
19. End-to-End Monitoring
Scope:
• Tracks integration issues, system health, and message flows.
• Monitors exceptions, performance, response times, and throughput.

Capabilities:
• Manages real time integration artifacts monitoring through end-to-end message flows.
• Provides alert notifications and analytics for issue resolution.
• Supports in various automations for operational tasks.

Monitoring Tools:
• SAP Cloud ALM: Tailored for cloud-based applications.
• SAP Solution Manager: Monitors on-premise systems.
• SAP Focused Run: High-performance monitoring for large-scale landscapes.

                     


________________________________________

SAP Integration Architecture Guide, provides a design framework for building a scalable and future-proof integration landscape. Using SAP's tools, templates, methodologies, and best practices, organizations can unlock new opportunities for innovation, operational efficiency, and business growth.

 

References:

https://help.sap.com/docs/sap-btp-guidance-framework/guidance-framework/what-is-sap-btp-guidance-framework?q=SAP+BTP+Guidance+Framework

https://help.sap.com/docs/sap-btp-guidance-framework/integration-architecture-guide/summary

https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blogs-by-sap/integration-architecture-guide-updated-version-available-now/ba-p/13930016

entry.title: Re: Extension ledgers in Public cloud
entry.content:
Hi Sanne,

you can create any number of extension ledgers on top of a standard ledger (so there’s no restriction). And you don’t have to create them during scoping but can do this anytime later.
If you want to know more about extension ledgers and about the differences regarding standard ledgers, feel free to have a look at the following SAP Help Portal documents:

  • Ledger
  • Extension Ledger
  • Extension Ledger Types

I hope this helps.

Best regards,

Verena

entry.title: Problems when exporting result in F110
entry.content:
Hello SAP Community,

I am not able to download the result of transaction F110 into a folder of a shared network.

Once a get the result in F110, I go to Menu -> List -> Export -> Local File... 

Then I click on the file extension

I select the folder in the shared network and the file name I want to replace or generate


And after click on "replace" or "generate" SAP isn't working anymore and crashes.

 

I can save the result on other devices (different to the shared network). It's like the machines is having trouble accessing that device, but I can manually create and modify files in this path using File explorer.
I have tried this on other machines with my own SAP account and it worked.
Other users who do not have this problem have tried it on my machine with their own SAP accounts and it has failed too.
 
I have also tried to add the path manually to the Security Configuration in SAP Logon options, but there was no luck

I wonder if anyone has any clues about what might be wrong here.

 

Thanks in advance

 

Kind regards

Maria

entry.title: SAP Fiori Inbox parameters configuration
entry.content:
Hi,

Is there a configuration step for SAP Fiori Inbox somewhere to achieve the following:

In Launchpad designer we can set parameters like listSize or showAdditionalAttributes. This works fine if users start the Fiori Inbox via FLP and navigate to the Inbox tile.

If users have bookmarks to the Fiori Inbox or if notifications are sent via eMail and contain just the URL sap/bc/ui5_ui5/ui2/ushell/shells/abap/FioriLaunchpad.html#WorkflowTask-displayInbox the parameters are not set and Inbox behaves differently.

So is there a "place" where Inbox parameters can be defined and Inbox uses the defined parameters independently if called via direct Link or via FLP?

Thanks for your input(s).

#Fiori #Inbox #FioriInbox

entry.title: Re: How to use DDCDS_CUSTOMER_DOMAIN_VALUE_T?
entry.content:
Hi,
This is a workaround provided by @developer_Shakya in the you tube video of this Link, since this Standard CDS is not working for many users.

Create a new CDS view with a parameter to fetch the Domain values

 

define view entity Z_DOMAIN_VALUE_T 
with parameters p_domain_name:  abap.char(30) --sxco_ad_object_name
as select from dd07t
{
    key domname as domain_name,
    key valpos as value_position,
    key ddlanguage as language,
    domvalue_l as Value,
    @Semantics.text: true
    ddtext as Description
}
where domname = $parameters.p_domain_name and dd07t.as4local = 'A'

entry.title: Re: In CIDS how does one merge data from 2 tables but hav...
entry.content:
Hi Micbur,

I am really late to the party here, but I searched looking for help (and there isn't much out there).  I have subsequently managed to get this working.

Add a Row Generator with a count of 2.

In table 1 add a field TheKey with a value of 1

In table 2 add a field TheKey with a value of 2

Join the 3 tables (Row generator and the 2 tables to merge).  Set two left outer joins with the row generator on the left the two tables to merge on the right and linking to the two TheKey fields to the generated number.

You can use IFTHENELSE to select the value from the correct table.

This works really nicely - it would be great if we had a Union command.

Regards,

Jez

entry.title: Re: Can time pairs be split across midnight in SuccessFac...
entry.content:
Hello MMILLER1112,

yes this is possibly. You can use time record filters to generate a public holiday premium for example only for the time slot 00:00 - 12:00pm. Or you can even generate a Sunday premium in a time period from Sunday 00:00 till Monday 06:00am for example. And even if the employee records on Monday from 05.00 am till 1:00pm working time you nevertheless can generate a 1 hour Sunday premium, although it is Calendar day monday. So, take a look on the time records filters and how they work, they are pretty flexible.

However, what you cannot do is split the record for a "complete" valuation on different days. But I guess this is anyway not what you want. So, example would be: the 2 hours 12/9/ count for an overtime calculation on 12/9 including all hours recorded on that day, and the hours on 12/10 are assigned to the next "logical" day and count for overtime calculation on 12/10. But as mentioned, I guess you just want a split for premium pay / wage type generation and this is possible.

Best regards

Volker

oSheet.build().finally(function() {

entry.title: New contract template is not determining in Contract Preview
entry.content:
At Form Template Maintenance you have defined a country-dependent new custom Contract form template "NEWNEWNEW" for country = US and language = English.

 

The standard logic to retrieve the country for the form template selection works as follows:

Step 1: Take the country of the Sales Unit of the Contract.
Step 2: If there is no Sales Unit, then take the country of the Sales Org of the Contract.
Step 3: If there is no Sales Org, then take the country of the Seller party of the Contract.

 

However, Contract 58397 has neither a Sales Unit, nor a Sales Org, nor a Seller party assigned. As a consequence the country is undefined and the expected form template is not determined, because it requires that the country is US.

 

Solution Alternatives:

Make sure that the Contract contains a Sales Unit, a Sales Org, or a Seller with country US.
At Form Template Maintenance upload your custom form template "NewNewNew" only with a language, but do not define a country (should remain empty).

 

Please note that the standard Contract form "Contract Summary" at Form Template Maintenance is only defined for different languages, but there exist no country-dependent versions - hence the country is empty. The reason for this is that the standard Contract form does not contain any country dependent address data - so that no different formatting dependent on the country is required; but a translation without re-formatting is sufficient.

entry.title: Re: How to Pass parameter value from MD04 to custom tcode
entry.content:
Hello @RaghavanKathamuthu 

You don't need to set values for material number, plant, MRP area, MRP controller and planning scenario. These are preset dynamically based on the current values in MD04 - include MM61RF90_GLOBAL_PID_SET:

FORM GLOBAL_PID_SET USING MATNR LIKE MARC-MATNR
                          WERKS LIKE MARC-WERKS
                          BERID LIKE RM61R-BERID
                          PLSCN LIKE RM61R-PLSCN
                          DISPO LIKE MDKP-DISPO.        "46c

*--> Material übergeben
  IF NOT MATNR IS INITIAL.
    SET PARAMETER ID 'MAT' FIELD MATNR.
  ENDIF.
*--> Werk übergeben
  IF NOT WERKS IS INITIAL.
    SET PARAMETER ID 'WRK' FIELD WERKS.
  ENDIF.
*--> Dispobereich übergeben
  IF NOT BERID IS INITIAL.
*    IF NOT T000MD-DISFG IS INITIAL.
      SET PARAMETER ID 'BERID' FIELD BERID.
*    ENDIF.
  ENDIF.
*--> Szenario übergeben
  IF NOT PLSCN IS INITIAL.
    SET PARAMETER ID 'PLS' FIELD PLSCN.
  ENDIF.
*--> Disponent übergeben     new 46c
  IF NOT DISPO IS INITIAL.
    SET PARAMETER ID 'DGR' FIELD DISPO.
  ENDIF.

ENDFORM.

Then a transaction defined in a navigation profile is called by CALL_TRANSACTION_ERP function. You can put a breakpoint there and see how PIDs are handled. Global PIDs are passed to the transaction as well as any additional PIDs defined in the navigation profile. Values of the additional PIDs can be static only, i.e. hard coded in the navigation profile configuration.

Unfortunately MDVP that you intend to call doesn't read PID WRK for production plant selection criterion. The PID is not defined for S_WERKS in MDVP - include RLD05TOP:

SELECTION-SCREEN BEGIN OF BLOCK bl2 WITH FRAME TITLE text-bl2.
SELECT-OPTIONS:
  s_plnum   FOR plsv-plnum,
  s_werks   FOR plaf-plwrk, "<--- missing MEMORY ID wrk
  s_dispo   FOR plaf-dispo,
  s_fevor   FOR plaf-plgrp,
  s_matnr   FOR plsv-matnr  MATCHCODE OBJECT mat1.
* Selektionskriterien für Serienaufträge
SELECT-OPTIONS so_verid FOR rm61e-verid.
sel_opt_mdv0x ra_mdv01 ra_mdv02.
SELECT-OPTIONS s_mdpbv   FOR plaf-mdpbv.
PARAMETERS pa_kapfx LIKE iocsel-av_kapfx.
PARAMETERS pa_peonl LIKE rm61e-kzpeonly.
PARAMETERS pa_nmont LIKE iocsel-av_no_mont.
SELECTION-SCREEN END OF BLOCK bl2.

S_WERKS misses MEMORY ID wrk addition. 

Therefore, you can't call MDVP from MD04 and pass plant value, unless you modify S_WERKS definition in MDVP and add the missing MEMORY ID addition.

Best regards

Dominik Tylczynski

entry.title: Overcoming the limitations of standard RAG approaches with advanced techniques
entry.content:
Retrieval Augmented Generation (RAG) has become an essential tactic for improving the performance of Large Language Models (LLMs) and represents a significant milestone in the AI journey we started about a year ago, and we have successfully implemented it in various customer use cases. However, the standard RAG approaches have their limitations, which the advanced RAG techniques aim to address. This blog post addresses these limitations and shows how advanced methods can provide robust solutions.Standard RAG Approach

Challenges with Standard RAG Approaches

  •  Simplicity and Limited Customization

The basic RAG approach, as shown in the first image, primarily involves a retrieval step, a prompt and an LLM. The system relies heavily on searching for top-k documents based on similarity scores. While this approach is simple, it offers limited customization options as only the value of k can be adjusted. This simplicity can lead to sub-optimal results, especially for complex queries that require a more differentiated search.

  •  The Lost-in-the-Middle Problem

A common issue with traditional RAG models is the ‘lost-in-the-middle’ problem, which is highlighted in a study by An et al. (2024) entitled ‘Make Your LLM Fully Utilize the Context’. Although LLMs process large inputs, they are often unable to utilize the full context effectively due to insufficient monitoring during training. This is reflected in performance degradation in document searches, code function searches and database entity searches where critical information is ignored, especially in the middle segments of the input data.

  •  Ineffective Information Utilization

As mentioned in the work of Guo et al. (2024), the traditional RAG process does not compress or organize information efficiently, which often results in less relevant data being processed, which can affect the performance of LLMs. This inefficiency emphasizes the need for a more sophisticated mechanism that can rank information based on its relevance and importance.

 

Advanced RAG Approaches: A Solution

  • Implementation of the LOTR Merger Retriever

A key solution to the lost-in-the-middle problem is the use of Lord of the Retriever (LOTR - pun intended), also known as MergerRetriever. LOTR improves the retrieval process by merging the results of multiple retrievers, ensuring that all sections of the input are considered and not just the beginning or end. This method prevents critical data, often found in the middle of the text, from being overlooked.

Similar to how we might remember the first and last items on a shopping list but forget the middle ones, traditional RAG systems fail to capture important details in the middle of a document. LOTR solves this problem by combining the results of different retrievers and prioritizing the most relevant documents. This leads to more balanced and accurate answers and ensures that no part of the data is overlooked, whether it is the beginning, middle or end.

By integrating LOTR into the RAG process, we improve the system's ability to process complex data sets and ensure a more comprehensive understanding of the information retrieved.

  • Information Compression with Ranking

The advanced RAG approach, which is inspired by innovative methods such as those of Guo et al. (2024), uses a ranking mechanism based on lossless data compression. In this way, the compression ratio and thus the performance of LLMs is significantly improved. A broader range of documents (top k: 20) is selected and narrowed down to the most relevant group (top k: 5) to ensure that only essential information is processed.

Information Compression with Ranking

 

  • Incorporation of an Evaluator

An additional layer of the advanced RAG model is the evaluator, which assigns points to documents based on their relevance and hallucination potential. This scoring system ensures that the responses generated closely match the relevant context and reduces possible inaccuracies, making the results of the LLM more reliable.

  •  Enhanced Customization and Control

In contrast to the basic approach, the advanced RAG model offers more parameters for customization - top_k_retrieval and top_K_ranker - and introduces rigorous evaluation metrics such as document relevance, hallucination and similarity scores. This flexibility enables a more tailored retrieval process that maximizes the utility and accuracy of LLM output.

Evaluation metrics

The evaluation of RAG outputs is crucial to understanding their effectiveness. As outlined in the RAG evaluation methods, metrics include:

- Response vs. reference response: Measures correctness and similarity to ground truth.

- Response vs. input context: Assesses the relevance and usefulness of the response.

- Response vs. Retrieved Documents: Assesses reliability and possible hallucinations.

These scoring protocols ensure that advanced RAG solutions not only retrieve the responses, but also put them in the right context to the user queries.

Advanced RAG Approach

Conclusion

Advanced RAG approaches represent a significant leap forward by addressing the inherent limitations of standard RAG models. By introducing compression-based ranking and improved evaluation metrics, these solutions promise a more efficient and accurate information retrieval process that increases the overall utility of LLMs in various applications.

As research continues, these advances will further refine the approach to large language models and ensure increasingly better results for complex data retrieval tasks.

entry.title: SAP Ariba Help Center – Supporto Fornitori
entry.content:
I fornitori SAP Ariba hanno la possibilita’ di essere richiamati dal team di supporto per ricevere assistenza, tale richiesta puo’ essere inoltrata attraverso la pagina di supporto SAP Ariba.

A seguito gli step da eseguire:

Raggiungere la pagina web https://support.ariba.com/help

Cio’ vi portera’ alla schermata sottostante (non sara’ necessario effettuare il Login)
Avrete la possibilita’ di cambiare la lingua pre-impostata usando l’icona in alto a destra

Selezionare una delle seguenti opzioni:

Avrete la possibilita’ di fornire una descrizione della vostra domanda e selezionare l’opzione di ricerca per accedere a numerosi articoli che potrebbero essere d’aiuto per la vostra richiesta.

Nel caso in cui gli articoli forniti non fossero inerenti alla vostra domanda, avrete la possibilita’ di parlare con uno degli agenti di supporto SAP cliccando

Contatti in alto a sinistra

Comparira’ la schermata sottostante, vi invitiamo a selezionare Guida all’accesso

 

 

Da qui vi sara’ presentata una nuova serie di opzioni tra le quali potrete selezionare Ho un problema diverso

 

Comparira’ l’opzione Crea un Caso

Compliate il modulo sottostante riempiendo le sezioni evidenziate con gli asterischi rossi e accettate i termini e condizioni in fondo alla pagina. Potrete selezionare la lingua nella quale preferite essere contattati.

 

Una volta inseriti I dettagli della richiesta potrete selezionare l’opzione Un ultimo passo in basso a destra.

Vedrete il simbolo di un telefono con il tempo di attesa stimato per la chiamata. Potrete decidere se registrare o meno la chiamata.

 

 

L’ultimo step per ricevere la chiamata dal team di supporto SAP Ariba per fornitori sara’ inviare il caso cliccando OK.

 

 

 SAP Ariba Buyers

Per il supporto Buyers, vi invitiamo ad accedere ad SAP for Me.

In caso di problematiche di accesso relative a SAP Ariba, vi invitiamo a contattare il vostro Designated Support Contact.

Il vostro Designated Support Contact puo’ essere individuato attraverso la sezione  Administration & More -> Contacts.

 3121688 - S-user ID authorizations for Designated Support Contact (DSC) access or Basic access - SAP...

Per richiedere assistenza al supporto SAP Ariba, potrete creare un caso su  SAP for Me.

E’ necessario possedere un’utenza S-User ID con le corrette autorizzazioni assegnate per poter creare un caso su SAP for ME.

Per imparare come creare un caso su SAP for ME, vi invitiamo a consultare gli step indicati qui  1296527 - How to create a support case for SAP Support - SAP for Me

Grazie per aver letto!

Ci auguriamo che sia stato d’aiuto. Vi preghiamo di notare che il Customer Interaction Center e’ sempre disponibile per guidarvi attraverso gli step sopra elencati.

Ci piacerebbe sapere il vostro parere, cosa ne pensate?

Consultate piu’ Customer Interaction Center blog Here!

Altri argomenti relativi

Vi invitiamo a consultare anche SAP Ariba Network for Suppliers: Frequently Asked Question 

How do I contact SAP Business Network Customer Support as a supplier?

Potrete richiedere una Demo o contattare il team vendite SAP Ariba here .
